title: Romance languages

infobox.value: Originated in [Old Latium](/wiki/Old_Latium "Old Latium") on the [Italian peninsula](/wiki/Italian_peninsula "Italian peninsula") Today spoken in [Latin Europe](/wiki/Languages_of_Europe#Romance "Languages of Europe") (parts of [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), and [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe")), much of [the Americas](/wiki/The_Americas "The Americas"), and parts of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), and [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania")

* [Italo-Western](/wiki/Italo-Western_languages "Italo-Western languages") * [Eastern Romance](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages") * [Southern Romance](/wiki/Southern_Romance_languages "Southern Romance languages") * [Pannonian](/wiki/Pannonian_Latin "Pannonian Latin") † * [British](/wiki/British_Latin "British Latin") †

Please help **[improve it](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Romance_languages "Special:EditPage/Romance languages")** or discuss these issues on the **[talk page](/wiki/Talk:Romance_languages "Talk:Romance languages")**. *([Learn how and when to remove these messages](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | This article **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(June 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | This article **should specify the language of its non-English content** using {{[lang](/wiki/Template:Lang "Template:Lang")}} or {{[langx](/wiki/Template:Langx "Template:Langx")}}, {{[transliteration](/wiki/Template:Transliteration "Template:Transliteration")}} for transliterated languages, and {{[IPA](/wiki/Template:IPA "Template:IPA")}} for phonetic transcriptions, with an appropriate [ISO 639 code](/wiki/Lists_of_ISO_639_codes "Lists of ISO 639 codes"). Wikipedia's [multilingual support templates](/wiki/Category:Wikipedia_multilingual_support_templates "Category:Wikipedia multilingual support templates") may also be used. [See why](/wiki/Template:Lang#Rationale "Template:Lang"). *(June 2020)* |  *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Direct descendants of Vulgar Latin

Not to be confused with [Love Language (disambiguation)](/wiki/Love_Language_(disambiguation) "Love Language (disambiguation)"), [Romance (love)](/wiki/Romance_(love) "Romance (love)"), or [Romansh language](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language").

This article contains **[phonetic transcriptions](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") in the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") (IPA)**. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). For the distinction between [ ], / / and ⟨ ⟩, see [IPA § Brackets and transcription delimiters](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet#Brackets_and_transcription_delimiters "International Phonetic Alphabet").

The **Romance languages**, also known as the **Latin**[[2]](#cite_note-2) or **Neo-Latin languages**,[[3]](#cite_note-3) are the [languages](/wiki/Language "Language") that [directly descended](/wiki/Language_family "Language family") from [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin").[[4]](#cite_note-4) They are the only extant subgroup of the [Italic branch](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages").

The five [most widely spoken Romance languages](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") by number of native speakers are:

* [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (489 million): official in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea") and [Hispanic America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America"); widely spoken in the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America")
* [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") (240 million):[[5]](#cite_note-5) official in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Portuguese-speaking Africa](/wiki/Portuguese-speaking_African_countries "Portuguese-speaking African countries"), [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau")
* [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (80 million): official in [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), and [21 other countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_French_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where French is an official language"), but majority native in fewer
* [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (67 million): official in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") and Switzerland; minority language in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"); regional in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") ([Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria")) and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") ([Santa Teresa, Espírito Santo](/wiki/Santa_Teresa,_Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Santa Teresa, Espírito Santo") and [Encantado, Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Encantado,_Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Encantado, Rio Grande do Sul"))[[6]](#cite_note-encantado-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") (25 million): official in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and the autonomous province of [Vojvodina](/wiki/Vojvodina "Vojvodina") in [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"); minority language in [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), the rest of Serbia and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine").

The Romance languages spread throughout the world owing to the period of [European colonialism](/wiki/European_colonialism "European colonialism") beginning in the 15th century. There are more than 900 million native speakers of Romance languages found worldwide, mainly in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and parts of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). French, Spanish, and Portuguese also have many non-native speakers; they are widely used as [lingua francas](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca").[[1]](#cite_note-ethnologue.com-1) There are also numerous [regional](/wiki/Regional_languages "Regional languages") Romance languages and dialects. All five of the most widely spoken Romance languages are also official languages of the European Union.

## Name and languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name and languages")]

The term *[Romance](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Romance "wikt:Romance")* derives from the Vulgar Latin adverb *romanice*, "in [Roman](/wiki/Rome "Rome")", derived from *romanicus*: for instance, in the expression *romanice loqui*, "to speak in Roman" (that is, the Latin [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular")), contrasted with *latine loqui*, "to speak in Latin" (Medieval Latin, the [conservative](/wiki/Linguistic_conservatism "Linguistic conservatism") version of the language used in [writing and formal contexts](/wiki/Abstand_and_ausbau_languages#Roofing "Abstand and ausbau languages") or as a lingua franca), and with *barbarice loqui*, "to speak in [Barbarian](/wiki/Barbarian "Barbarian")" (the non-Latin languages of the peoples living outside the Roman Empire).[[9]](#cite_note-Ilari-9) From this adverb the noun *romance* originated, which applied initially to anything written *romanice*, or "in the Roman vernacular".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Most of the Romance-speaking part of Europe has traditionally been a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum"), where the speech variety of a location differs only slightly from that of a neighboring location, but over a longer distance these differences become so great that people from two remote locations unambiguously speak separate languages. This makes drawing language boundaries difficult, and thus there is no unambiguous way to divide the Romance varieties into individual languages. Even the criterion of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") can become ambiguous when it comes to determining whether two language varieties belong to the same language or not.[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica_Online-11)

The following is a list of groupings of Romance languages, with some languages chosen to exemplify each grouping. Not all languages are listed, and the groupings should not be interpreted as well-separated [genetic clades](/wiki/Cladistics#In_disciplines_other_than_biology "Cladistics") in a [tree model](/wiki/Tree_model "Tree model"):

* [Ibero-Romance](/wiki/Ibero-Romance_languages "Ibero-Romance languages"): [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language"), [Asturleonese](/wiki/Asturleonese_language "Asturleonese language") (including [Mirandese](/wiki/Mirandese_language "Mirandese language")), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Aragonese](/wiki/Aragonese_language "Aragonese language"), [Judaeo-Spanish](/wiki/Judaeo-Spanish "Judaeo-Spanish"), [Mozarabic/Andalusi Romance](/wiki/Mozarabic_language "Mozarabic language") (extinct since the late 13th century);
* [Occitano-Romance](/wiki/Occitano-Romance_languages "Occitano-Romance languages"): [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language")/[Valencian](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language"), [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language")
* [Gallo-Romance](/wiki/Gallo-Romance_languages "Gallo-Romance languages"): [Oïl languages](/wiki/Langues_d%27o%C3%AFl "Langues d'oïl") (including [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")), [Franco-Provençal (Arpitan)](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language");
* [Rhaeto-Romance](/wiki/Rhaeto-Romance_languages "Rhaeto-Romance languages"): [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language"), [Ladin](/wiki/Ladin_language "Ladin language"), [Friulian](/wiki/Friulian_language "Friulian language");
* [Cisalpine](/wiki/Cisalpine_languages "Cisalpine languages"): [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language"), [Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_(Romance_language) "Ligurian (Romance language)"), [Lombard](/wiki/Lombard_language "Lombard language"), [Emilian](/wiki/Emilian_dialects "Emilian dialects"), [Romagnol](/wiki/Romagnol_dialects "Romagnol dialects");
* [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language") (classification disputed);
* [Central Romance](/wiki/Central_Romance_languages "Central Romance languages"): [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") ([Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect"), [Corsican](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language"), [Sassarese](/wiki/Sassarese_language "Sassarese language"), [Central Italian](/wiki/Central_Italian "Central Italian")), [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language")/[Extreme Southern Italian](/wiki/Southern_Italian_languages "Southern Italian languages"), [Neapolitan](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language")/[Southern Italian](/wiki/Southern_Italian_languages "Southern Italian languages"), [Dalmatian](/wiki/Dalmatian_language "Dalmatian language") (extinct in 1898), [Istriot](/wiki/Istriot_language "Istriot language");
* [Eastern Romance](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages"): [Daco-Romanian (Romanian)](/wiki/Daco-Romanian_language "Daco-Romanian language"), [Aromanian](/wiki/Aromanian_language "Aromanian language"), [Megleno-Romanian](/wiki/Megleno-Romanian_language "Megleno-Romanian language"), [Istro-Romanian](/wiki/Istro-Romanian_language "Istro-Romanian language");
* [Southern Romance](/wiki/Southern_Romance_languages "Southern Romance languages"): [African](/wiki/African_Romance_language "African Romance language") (extinct since the Late Middle Ages), [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") ([Campidanese](/wiki/Campidanese_Sardinian "Campidanese Sardinian"), [Logudorese](/wiki/Logudorese_Sardinian "Logudorese Sardinian"));
* [Southern Lucanian](/wiki/Southern_Lucanian_dialects "Southern Lucanian dialects") (classification disputed);

## Modern status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modern status")]

Main articles: [Romance-speaking Europe](/wiki/Romance-speaking_Europe "Romance-speaking Europe"), [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), and [Latin Union](/wiki/Latin_Union "Latin Union")

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(March 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Romance_20c_en.png/500px-Romance_20c_en.png)](/wiki/File:Romance_20c_en.png)

European extent of Romance languages in the 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/2024_Updated_Total_Speakers.jpg/500px-2024_Updated_Total_Speakers.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024_Updated_Total_Speakers.jpg)

Proportion of speakers in the top 5 Romance languages, as of 2024

The Romance language [most widely spoken natively](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") today is [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), followed by [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), which together cover a vast territory in Europe and beyond, and work as [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and [national languages](/wiki/National_language "National language") in dozens of countries.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkireRosen20101–4-12) In Europe, at least one Romance language is official in [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). In these countries, French, Portuguese, Italian, Spanish, Romanian, [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language") and [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") have constitutional official status.

French, Italian, Portuguese, Spanish, and Romanian are also official languages of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Spanish, Portuguese, French, Italian, Romanian, and Catalan were the official languages of the defunct [Latin Union](/wiki/Latin_Union "Latin Union");[[16]](#cite_note-16) and French and Spanish are two of the six official languages of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Outside Europe, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") are spoken and enjoy official status in various countries that emerged from the respective [colonial empires](/wiki/Colonial_empire "Colonial empire").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

With almost 500 million speakers worldwide, [Spanish is an official language](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Spanish_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Spanish is an official language") in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and in nine countries of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), home to about half that continent's population; in six countries of [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") (all except [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize")); and in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"). In the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), it is official in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), and [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"). In all these countries and territories, [Latin American Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language_in_the_Americas "Spanish language in the Americas") is the vernacular language of the majority of the population, giving Spanish the most native speakers of any Romance language. In Africa, Spanish is one of the official languages of [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea"). Spanish was one of the official languages in the Philippines in Southeast Asia until 1973. In the 1987 constitution, Spanish was removed as an official language (replaced by English), and was listed as an optional language.

Portuguese, in its homeland, [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), is spoken by almost the entire population of 10 million. As the official language of [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), it is spoken by more than 200 million people, making Portuguese the most spoken [official Romance language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") in a single country. As Portuguese is also spoken in neighboring [eastern Paraguay](/wiki/Brasiguayos "Brasiguayos") and [northern Uruguay](/wiki/Riverense_Portu%C3%B1ol_language "Riverense Portuñol language"), this accounts for slightly more than half the population of South America.

Portuguese is the official language of six African countries ([Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola"), [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), [Guinea-Bissau](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau "Guinea-Bissau"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea"), and [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe")), and is spoken as a native language by perhaps 16 million residents of that continent.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[*[unreliable source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] In Asia, Portuguese is co-official with other languages in [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), while most Portuguese-speakers in Asia—some 400,000[[22]](#cite_note-22)—are in [Japan](/wiki/Japan#Demographics "Japan") due to [return immigration](/wiki/Dekasegi "Dekasegi") of [Japanese Brazilians](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilian "Japanese Brazilian"). In North America 1,000,000 people speak Portuguese as their home language, mainly immigrants from Brazil, Portugal, and other Portuguese-speaking countries and their descendants.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In Oceania, Portuguese is the second most spoken Romance language, after French, due mainly to the number of speakers in [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor"). Its closest relative, Galician, has co-official status in the [autonomous community](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_in_Spain "Autonomous communities in Spain") of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), together with Spanish.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Outside Europe, French is spoken natively most in the Canadian province of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), and in parts of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"). Canada is [officially bilingual](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada"), with French and English being the official languages and government services in French theoretically mandated to be provided nationwide. In parts of the Caribbean, such as [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), French has official status, but most people speak [creoles](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language") such as [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole") as their native language. French also has official status in much of Africa, with relatively few native speakers but large numbers of second language speakers.

Although [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") also had some colonial possessions before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), its language did not remain official after the end of the colonial period. As a result, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") outside Italy and Switzerland is now spoken only as a minority language by immigrant communities in [North](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). In some former Italian colonies in Africa—namely [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya"), [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") and [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia")—it is spoken by a few educated people in commerce and government.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") did not establish a colonial empire. The native range of Romanian includes [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), where it is the dominant language and spoken by a majority of the population, and neighboring areas in Serbia ([Vojvodina](/wiki/Vojvodina "Vojvodina") and the [Bor District](/wiki/Bor_District "Bor District")), Bulgaria, Hungary, and Ukraine ([Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), [Budjak](/wiki/Budjak "Budjak")) and in some villages between the [Dniester](/wiki/Dniester "Dniester") and [Bug](/wiki/Bug_River "Bug River") rivers.[[25]](#cite_note-25) As with Italian, Romanian is spoken outside its ethnic range by immigrant communities. In Europe, Romanian speakers form about two percent of the population in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"). Romanian is also spoken in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") by Romanian Jews,[[26]](#cite_note-26) where it is the native language of five percent of the population,[[27]](#cite_note-27) and is spoken by many more as a secondary language. The [Aromanian language](/wiki/Aromanian_language "Aromanian language") is spoken today by [Aromanians](/wiki/Aromanians "Aromanians") in Bulgaria, North Macedonia, Albania, Kosovo, and Greece.[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Flavio Biondo](/wiki/Flavio_Biondo "Flavio Biondo") was the first scholar to have observed (in 1435) linguistic affinities between the Romanian and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") languages, as well as their common Latin origin.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The total of 880 million native speakers of Romance languages (ca. 2020) are divided as follows:[[30]](#cite_note-ethnologue1-30)

* [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") 54% (475 million, plus 75 million L2 for 550 million in the [Hispanophones](/wiki/Hispanophone "Hispanophone"))
* [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") 26% (230 million, plus 30 million L2 for 260 million in the [Lusophones](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone"))
* [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") 9% (80 million, plus 230 million L2 for 310 million in the [Francophones](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone"))
* [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") 7% (65 million, plus 3 million L2)
* [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") 3% (24 million)
* [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") 0.5% (4 million, plus 5 million L2)
* Others 3% (26 million, nearly all bilingual in one of the national languages)

Catalan is the official language of [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"). In Spain, it is co-official with Spanish in [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), the [Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community") (under the name [Valencian](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language")), and the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands"), and it is recognized, but not official, in an area of [Aragon](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon") known as [La Franja](/wiki/La_Franja "La Franja"). In addition, it is spoken by many residents of [Alghero](/wiki/Alghero "Alghero"), on the island of [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") and is co-official in that city.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELedgewayMaiden2016393-31) [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language"), with more than three million speakers, is official together with Spanish in [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"), and has legal recognition in neighbouring territories in [Castilla y León](/wiki/Castilla_y_Le%C3%B3n "Castilla y León"). A few other languages have official recognition on a regional or otherwise limited level; for instance, [Asturian](/wiki/Asturian_language "Asturian language") and [Aragonese](/wiki/Aragonese_language "Aragonese language") in Spain; [Mirandese](/wiki/Mirandese_language "Mirandese language") in Portugal; [Friulian](/wiki/Friulan_language "Friulan language"), [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") and [Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language") in Italy; and [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language") in Switzerland.[*[This paragraph needs citation(s)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The remaining Romance languages survive mostly as spoken languages for informal contact. National governments have historically viewed linguistic diversity as an economic, administrative or military liability, as well as a potential source of [separatist](/wiki/Separatism "Separatism") movements; therefore, they have generally fought to eliminate it, by extensively promoting the use of the official language, restricting the use of the other languages in the media, recognizing them as mere "dialects", or even persecuting them. As a result, all of these languages are considered endangered to varying degrees according to the UNESCO [Red Book of Endangered Languages](/wiki/Red_Book_of_Endangered_Languages "Red Book of Endangered Languages"), ranging from "vulnerable" (e.g. [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language") and [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language")) to "severely endangered" ([Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language"), most of the [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") varieties). Since the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries, increased sensitivity to the rights of minorities has allowed some of these languages to start recovering their prestige and lost rights. Yet it is unclear whether these political changes will be enough to reverse the decline of minority Romance languages.[*[This paragraph needs citation(s)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Between 350 BC and 150 AD, [the expansion of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Campaign_history_of_the_Roman_military "Campaign history of the Roman military"), together with its administrative and educational policies, made Latin the dominant native language in continental Western Europe. Latin also exerted a strong influence in [southeastern Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), [the Roman province of Africa](/wiki/Africa_(Roman_province) "Africa (Roman province)"), [western Germany](/wiki/Moselle_Romance "Moselle Romance"), [Pannonia](/wiki/Pannonian_Romance "Pannonian Romance") and the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

During the empire's decline, and after its fragmentation and the collapse of its Western half in the fifth and sixth centuries, spoken varieties of Latin became more isolated from each other, with the western [dialects](/wiki/Dialects_of_Latin "Dialects of Latin") coming under heavy Germanic influence (the Goths and Franks in particular) and the eastern dialects coming under Slavic influence.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) The dialects diverged from Latin at an accelerated rate and eventually evolved into a continuum of recognizably different typologies. The colonial empires established by [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), and [France](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire") from the fifteenth century onward spread their languages to the other continents to such an extent that about two-thirds of all Romance language speakers today live outside Europe.

Despite other influences (e.g. *[substratum](/wiki/Stratum_(linguistics)#Substratum "Stratum (linguistics)")* from pre-Roman languages, especially [Continental Celtic languages](/wiki/Continental_Celtic_languages "Continental Celtic languages"); and *[superstratum](/wiki/Stratum_(linguistics)#Superstratum "Stratum (linguistics)")* from later [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") or [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") invasions), the [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology"), [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)"), and [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") of all Romance languages consist mainly of evolved forms of Vulgar Latin. However, some notable differences exist between today's Romance languages and their Roman ancestor. With only one or two exceptions, Romance languages have dropped the Latin [inflection](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") system, instead making extensive use of [prepositions](/wiki/Prepositions "Prepositions"). Romance languages also normally have [SVO](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") sentence structure.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDragomirescuNicolae2016911–914-35) By most measures, [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") and Italian are the least divergent languages from Latin, while French has changed the most.[[36]](#cite_note-36) However, all Romance languages are closer to each other than to [classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin").[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Vulgar Latin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Vulgar Latin")]

Main article: [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Map_Length_of_Roman_Rule_Neo_Latin_Languages.jpg/500px-Map_Length_of_Roman_Rule_Neo_Latin_Languages.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_Length_of_Roman_Rule_Neo_Latin_Languages.jpg)

Duration of Roman rule and the spread of the Romance languages[[39]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Latin_Europe.png/500px-Latin_Europe.png)](/wiki/File:Latin_Europe.png)

Romance languages in Europe

Documentary evidence about Vulgar Latin for the purposes of comprehensive research is limited, and the literature is often hard to interpret or generalize. Many of its speakers were soldiers, slaves, displaced peoples, and forced resettlers, and more likely to be natives of conquered lands than natives of Rome. In Western Europe, Latin gradually replaced [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") and other [Italic languages](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages"), which were related to it by a shared Indo-European origin. Commonalities in syntax and vocabulary facilitated the adoption of Latin.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-curchin-42)

To some scholars, this suggests the form of Vulgar Latin that evolved into the Romance languages was around during the time of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") (from the end of the first century BC), and was spoken alongside the written Classical Latin which was reserved for official and formal occasions. Other scholars argue that the distinctions are more rightly viewed as indicative of sociolinguistic and register differences normally found within any language. With the rise of the Roman Empire, spoken Latin spread first throughout Italy and then through [southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), [western](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), [central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), and [southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeastern_Europe "Southeastern Europe"), and [northern Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") along parts of [western Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia").[[43]](#cite_note-HarrisVincent2001-43): 1

Latin reached a stage when innovations became generalised around the sixth and seventh centuries.[[44]](#cite_note-Banniard-44) After that time and within two hundred years, it became a [dead language](/wiki/Language_death "Language death") since "the Romanized people of Europe could no longer understand texts that were read aloud or recited to them."[[45]](#cite_note-45) By the eighth and ninth centuries Latin gave way to Romance.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanniard201395-46)

### Fall of the Western Roman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fall of the Western Roman Empire")]

During the political [decline of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") in the fifth century, there were large-scale [migrations](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") into the empire, and the Latin-speaking world was fragmented into several independent states. Central Europe and the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") were occupied by Germanic and [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") tribes, as well as by [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns").

[British](/wiki/British_Latin "British Latin") and [African Romance](/wiki/African_Romance "African Romance")—the forms of Vulgar Latin used in [Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") and [the Roman province of Africa](/wiki/Africa_(Roman_province) "Africa (Roman province)"), where it had been spoken by much of the urban population—disappeared in the Middle Ages (as did [Moselle Romance](/wiki/Moselle_Romance "Moselle Romance") in Germany). But the Germanic tribes that had penetrated [Roman Italy](/wiki/Roman_Italy "Roman Italy"), [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), and [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania") eventually adopted Latin/Romance and the remnants of the [culture of ancient Rome](/wiki/Culture_of_ancient_Rome "Culture of ancient Rome") alongside existing inhabitants of those regions, and so Latin remained the dominant language there. In part due to regional dialects of the Latin language and local environments, several languages evolved from it.[[43]](#cite_note-HarrisVincent2001-43): 4

### Fall of the Eastern Roman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Fall of the Eastern Roman Empire")]

Meanwhile, large-scale [migrations](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") into the Eastern [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") started with the [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths") and continued with [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"), [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars"), [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars"), [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"), [Pechenegs](/wiki/Pechenegs "Pechenegs"), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") and [Cumans](/wiki/Cumans "Cumans"). The invasions of [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") were the most thoroughgoing, and they partially reduced the Romanic element in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans").[[47]](#cite_note-47) The invasion of the [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and conquest of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") in 1453 marked the end of the empire.

The surviving local Romance languages were [Dalmatian](/wiki/Dalmatian_language "Dalmatian language") and [Common Romanian](/wiki/Common_Romanian "Common Romanian").

### Early Romance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early Romance")]

See also: [Lexical changes from Classical Latin to Proto-Romance](/wiki/Lexical_changes_from_Classical_Latin_to_Proto-Romance "Lexical changes from Classical Latin to Proto-Romance")

Over the course of the fourth to eighth centuries, local changes in phonology, morphology, syntax and lexicon accumulated to the point that the speech of any locale was noticeably different from that of another. In principle, differences between any two [lects](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") increased the more they were separated geographically, reducing easy mutual intelligibility between speakers of distant communities.[[48]](#cite_note-price-48) Clear evidence of some levels of change is found in the *[Reichenau Glossary](/wiki/Reichenau_Glossary "Reichenau Glossary")*, an eighth-century compilation of about 1,200 words from the fourth-century [Vulgate](/wiki/Vulgate "Vulgate") of [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome") that had changed in phonological form or were no longer normally used, along with their eighth-century equivalents in proto-[Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language").[[49]](#cite_note-49) The following are some examples with reflexes in several modern Romance languages for comparison:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

In all of the above examples, the words appearing in the fourth century Vulgate are the same words as would have been used in [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") of c. 50 BC. It is likely that some of these words had already disappeared from casual speech by the time of the *Glosses*; but if so, they may well have been still widely understood, as there is no recorded evidence that the common people of the time had difficulty understanding the language. By the 8th century, the situation was very different. During the late 8th century, [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), holding that "Latin of his age was by classical standards intolerably corrupt",[[48]](#cite_note-price-48): 6 successfully imposed Classical Latin as an artificial written vernacular for [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"). Unfortunately, this meant that parishioners could no longer understand the sermons of their priests, forcing the [Council of Tours in 813](/wiki/Council_of_Tours_813 "Council of Tours 813") to issue an edict that priests needed to translate their speeches into the *rustica romana lingua*, an explicit acknowledgement of the reality of the Romance languages as separate languages from Latin.[[48]](#cite_note-price-48): 6

By this time, and possibly as early as the 6th century according to Price (1984),[[48]](#cite_note-price-48): 6 the Romance [lects](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") had split apart enough to be able to speak of separate [Gallo-Romance](/wiki/Gallo-Romance_languages "Gallo-Romance languages"), [Ibero-Romance](/wiki/Iberian_Romance_languages "Iberian Romance languages"), [Italo-Romance](/wiki/Italo-Dalmatian_languages "Italo-Dalmatian languages") and [Eastern Romance languages](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages"). Some researchers[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] have postulated that the major divergences in the spoken dialects began or accelerated considerably in the 5th century, as the formerly widespread and efficient communication networks of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") rapidly broke down, leading to the total disappearance of the Western Roman Empire by the end of the century. During the period between the 5th–10th centuries AD Romance vernaculars documentation is scarce as the normal writing language used was [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin"), with vernacular writing only beginning in earnest in the 11th or 12th century. The earliest such texts are the [Indovinello Veronese](/wiki/Indovinello_veronese "Indovinello veronese") from the eight century and the [Oaths of Strasbourg](/wiki/Oaths_of_Strasbourg "Oaths of Strasbourg") from the second half of the ninth century.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Recognition of the vernaculars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Recognition of the vernaculars")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Germanic_%E2%80%93_Romance_language_border_map_%28early_Middle_Ages_%E2%80%93_early_twentieth_century%29.svg/500px-Germanic_%E2%80%93_Romance_language_border_map_%28early_Middle_Ages_%E2%80%93_early_twentieth_century%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Germanic_%E2%80%93_Romance_language_border_map_(early_Middle_Ages_%E2%80%93_early_twentieth_century).svg)

Romance – Germanic language border:[[54]](#cite_note-54)   
 • Early Middle Ages     
 • Early Twentieth Century

From the 10th century onwards, some local [vernaculars](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") developed a written form and began to supplant Latin in many of its roles.[[55]](#cite_note-Wright-55) In some countries, such as [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), this transition was expedited by force of law; whereas in others, such as [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), many prominent poets and writers used the vernacular of their own accord – some of the most famous in Italy being [Giacomo da Lentini](/wiki/Giacomo_da_Lentini "Giacomo da Lentini") and [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"). Well before that, the vernacular was also used for practical purposes, such as the testimonies in the [Placiti Cassinesi](/wiki/Placiti_Cassinesi "Placiti Cassinesi"), written 960–963.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Uniformization and standardization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Uniformization and standardization")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Romance_languages "Special:EditPage/Romance languages") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(March 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The invention of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") brought a tendency towards greater uniformity of [standard languages](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") within political boundaries, at the expense of other Romance languages and [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") less favored politically. In France, for instance, the dialect spoken in the region of Paris gradually spread to the entire country, and the [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") of the south lost ground.

Some of the divergence comes from [semantic change](/wiki/Semantic_change "Semantic change"): where the same root words have developed different meanings. For example, the Portuguese word *fresta* is descended from Latin *fenestra* "window" (and is thus [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") to French *fenêtre*, Italian *finestra*, Romanian *fereastră* and so on), but now means "skylight" and "slit". Cognates may exist but have become rare, such as *hiniestra* in Spanish, or dropped out of use entirely. The Spanish and Portuguese terms *defenestrar* meaning "to [throw something or someone through a window](/wiki/Defenestration "Defenestration")" and *fenestrado* meaning "replete with windows" also have the same root, but are later borrowings from Latin.

Likewise, Portuguese also has the word *cear*, a cognate of Italian *cenare* and Spanish *cenar*, but uses it in the sense of "to have a late supper" in most varieties, while the preferred word for "to dine" is *jantar* (related to archaic Spanish *yantar* "to eat") because of semantic changes in the 19th century. Galician has both *fiestra* (from medieval *fẽestra*, the ancestor of standard Portuguese *fresta*) and the less frequently used *ventá* and *xanela*.

As an alternative to *lei* (originally the genitive form), Italian has the pronoun *ella*, a cognate of the other words for "she", but it is hardly ever used in speaking.

Spanish, Asturian, and Leonese *ventana* and Mirandese and Sardinian *bentana* come from Latin *ventus* "wind" (cf. English *window*, etymologically 'wind eye'), and Portuguese *janela*, Galician *xanela*, Mirandese *jinela* from Latin \**ianuella* "small opening", a derivative of *ianua* "door".

Sardinian *balcone* (alternative for *ventàna*/*bentàna*) comes from Old Italian and is similar to other Romance languages such as French *balcon* (from Italian *balcone*), Portuguese *balcão*, Romanian *balcon*, Spanish *balcón*, Catalan *balcó* and Corsican *balconi* (alternative for *purtellu*).

## Classification and related languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Classification and related languages")]

Main article: [Classification of Romance languages](/wiki/Classification_of_Romance_languages "Classification of Romance languages")

Along with Latin and a few extinct languages of ancient Italy, the Romance languages make up the [Italic branch](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages") of the [Indo-European family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages").[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica_Online-11) Identifying subdivisions of the Romance languages is inherently problematic, because most of the linguistic area is a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum"), and in some cases political biases can come into play. A tree model is often used, but the selection of criteria results in different trees. Most classification schemes are, implicitly or not, historical and geographic, resulting in groupings such as [Ibero-](/wiki/Iberian_Romance_languages "Iberian Romance languages") and [Gallo-Romance](/wiki/Gallo-Romance_languages "Gallo-Romance languages"). A major division can be drawn between Eastern and Western Romance, separated by the [La Spezia-Rimini line](/wiki/La_Spezia%E2%80%93Rimini_Line "La Spezia–Rimini Line").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Idiomas_y_dialectos_romances.png/500px-Idiomas_y_dialectos_romances.png)](/wiki/File:Idiomas_y_dialectos_romances.png)

Romance languages and dialects

The main subfamilies that have been proposed by [Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue") within the various classification schemes for Romance languages are:[[57]](#cite_note-57)

* [Italo-Western](/wiki/Italo-Western_languages "Italo-Western languages"), the largest group, which includes languages such as Galician, Catalan, Portuguese, Italian, Spanish, and French.
* [Eastern Romance](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages"), which includes Romanian and closely related languages.
* [Southern Romance](/wiki/Southern_Romance_languages "Southern Romance languages"), which includes Sardinian and Corsican (even if Corsican is considered by some linguists to be a form of [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect"), so Italo-Western). This family is thought to have included the now-vanished Romance languages of [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") (or at least, they appear to have evolved some phonological features and their vowels in the same way).

### Ranking by distance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ranking by distance")]

Another approach involves attempts to rank the distance of Romance languages from each other or from their common ancestor (i.e. ranking languages based on how [conservative or innovative](/wiki/Conservative_and_innovative_language "Conservative and innovative language") they are, although the same language may be conservative in some respects while innovative in others). By most measures, French is the most highly differentiated Romance language, although Romanian has changed the greatest amount of its vocabulary, while Italian[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) and Sardinian have changed the least. Standard Italian can be considered a "central" language, which is generally somewhat easy to understand to speakers of other Romance languages, whereas French and Romanian are peripheral and quite dissimilar from the rest of Romance.[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica_Online-11)

### Pidgins, creoles, and mixed languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Pidgins, creoles, and mixed languages")]

Some Romance languages have developed varieties which seem dramatically restructured as to their grammars or to be mixtures with other languages. There are several dozens of creoles of [French](/wiki/French-based_creole_languages "French-based creole languages"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish-based_creole_languages "Spanish-based creole languages"), and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese-based_creole_languages "Portuguese-based creole languages") origin, some of them spoken as [national languages](/wiki/National_language "National language") and lingua franca in former European colonies.

**Creoles of French:**

* [Antillean](/wiki/Antillean_Creole_French "Antillean Creole French") ([French Antilles](/wiki/French_Antilles "French Antilles"), [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucian_Creole_French "Saint Lucian Creole French"), [Dominica](/wiki/Dominican_Creole_French "Dominican Creole French"); majority native language)
* [French Guianese](/wiki/French_Guianese_Creole "French Guianese Creole") (native language of [French Guiana](/wiki/French_Guiana "French Guiana"))
* [Haitian](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole") (one of [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti")'s two official languages and majority native language)
* [Karipúna](/wiki/Karip%C3%BAna_French_Creole "Karipúna French Creole") (regional language in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"))
* [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_Creole_French "Louisiana Creole French") (US)
* [Mauritian](/wiki/Mauritian_Creole "Mauritian Creole") (*[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"))
* [Réunion](/wiki/R%C3%A9union_Creole "Réunion Creole") (native language of [Réunion](/wiki/R%C3%A9union "Réunion"))
* [Seychellois](/wiki/Seychellois_Creole "Seychellois Creole") ([Seychelles](/wiki/Seychelles "Seychelles")' official language)
* [Tayo](/wiki/Tayo_Creole "Tayo Creole") (regional language in [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia"))

**Creoles of Spanish:**

* [Chavacano](/wiki/Chavacano "Chavacano") (in part of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"))
* [Palenquero](/wiki/Palenquero "Palenquero") (in part of [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"))

**Creoles of Portuguese:**

* [Angolar](/wiki/Angolar_Creole "Angolar Creole") (regional language in [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe"))
* [Cape Verdean](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Creole "Cape Verdean Creole") ([Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde")'s national language and lingua franca; includes several distinct varieties)
* [Daman and Diu Creole](/wiki/Daman_and_Diu_Portuguese_Creole "Daman and Diu Portuguese Creole") (regional language in [India](/wiki/India "India"))
* [Forro](/wiki/Forro_Creole "Forro Creole") (regional language in [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe"))
* [Guinea-Bissau Creole](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau_Creole "Guinea-Bissau Creole") ([Guinea-Bissau](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau "Guinea-Bissau")'s national language and lingua franca)
* [Kristang](/wiki/Kristang_language "Kristang language") ([Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"))
* [Kristi](/wiki/Kristi_language "Kristi language") (regional language in [India](/wiki/India "India"))
* [Macanese](/wiki/Macanese_Patois "Macanese Patois") ([Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"))
* [Papiamento](/wiki/Papiamento "Papiamento") ([Dutch Antilles](/wiki/Dutch_Antilles "Dutch Antilles") official language, majority native language, and lingua franca)

### Auxiliary and constructed languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Auxiliary and constructed languages")]

Main articles: [Constructed language](/wiki/Constructed_language "Constructed language") and [International auxiliary language](/wiki/International_auxiliary_language "International auxiliary language")

Latin and the Romance languages have also served as the inspiration and basis of numerous auxiliary and constructed languages, so-called "Neo-Romance languages".[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

The concept was first developed in 1903 by Italian mathematician [Giuseppe Peano](/wiki/Giuseppe_Peano "Giuseppe Peano"), under the title [Latino sine flexione](/wiki/Latino_sine_flexione "Latino sine flexione").[[63]](#cite_note-peano-de-63) He wanted to create a *naturalistic* international language, as opposed to an autonomous constructed language like [Esperanto](/wiki/Esperanto "Esperanto") or [Volapük](/wiki/Volap%C3%BCk "Volapük") which were designed for maximal simplicity of lexicon and derivation of words. Peano used Latin as the base of his language because, as he described it, Latin had been the international scientific language until the end of the 18th century.[[63]](#cite_note-peano-de-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Other languages developed include [Idiom Neutral](/wiki/Idiom_Neutral "Idiom Neutral") (1902), [Interlingue](/wiki/Interlingue "Interlingue")-Occidental (1922), [Interlingua](/wiki/Interlingua "Interlingua") (1951) and [Lingua Franca Nova](/wiki/Lingua_Franca_Nova "Lingua Franca Nova") (1998). The most famous and successful of these is Interlingua.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Each of these languages has attempted to varying degrees to achieve a pseudo-Latin vocabulary as common as possible to living Romance languages. Some languages have been constructed specifically for communication among speakers of Romance languages, the [Pan-Romance languages](/wiki/Pan-Romance_language "Pan-Romance language").

There are also languages created for artistic purposes only, such as [Talossan](/wiki/Talossa#Talossan_language "Talossa"). Because Latin is a very well attested ancient language, some amateur linguists have even constructed Romance languages that mirror real languages that developed from other ancestral languages. These include [Brithenig](/wiki/Brithenig "Brithenig") (which mirrors [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language")), Breathanach[[65]](#cite_note-65) (mirrors [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language")), [Wenedyk](/wiki/Wenedyk "Wenedyk") (mirrors [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language")), Þrjótrunn (mirrors [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language")),[[66]](#cite_note-66) and Helvetian (mirrors [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")).[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Consonants")]

Significant [sound changes](/wiki/Sound_change "Sound change") affected the consonants of the Romance languages.

#### Apocope

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Apocope")]

There was a tendency to eliminate final consonants in Vulgar Latin, either by dropping them ([apocope](/wiki/Apocope "Apocope")) or adding a vowel after them ([epenthesis](/wiki/Epenthesis "Epenthesis")).

Many final consonants were rare, occurring only in certain prepositions (e.g. *ad* "towards", *apud* "at, near (a person)"), conjunctions (*sed* "but"), demonstratives (e.g. *illud* "that (over there)", *hoc* "this"), and nominative singular noun forms, especially of neuter nouns (e.g. *lac* "milk", *mel* "honey", *cor* "heart"). Many of these prepositions and conjunctions were replaced by others, while the nouns were regularized into forms based on their oblique stems that avoided the final consonants (e.g. \**lacte*, \**mele*, \**core*).

Final *-m* was dropped in Vulgar Latin.[[68]](#cite_note-Gabriel,_Gess,_Meisenburg-68) Even in [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin"), final *-am*, *-em*, *-um* were often [elided](/wiki/Elision "Elision") in [poetic meter](/wiki/Meter_(poetry) "Meter (poetry)"), suggesting the *m* was weakly pronounced, probably marking the [nasalisation](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel") of the vowel before it. This nasal vowel lost its nasalization in the Romance languages except in monosyllables, where it became /n/ e.g. Spanish *quien* < *quem* "whom",[[68]](#cite_note-Gabriel,_Gess,_Meisenburg-68) French *rien* "anything" < *rem* "thing";[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyd-Bowman1980133-69) note especially French and Catalan *mon* < *meum* "my (m.sg.)" which are derived from monosyllabic /meu̯m/ > \*/meu̯n/, /mun/, whereas Spanish disyllabic *mío* and Portuguese and Catalan monosyllabic *meu* are derived from disyllabic /ˈme.um/ > \*/ˈmeo/.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a result, only the following final consonants occurred in Vulgar Latin:

* Final *-t* in third-person singular verb forms, and *-nt* (later reduced in many languages to *-n*) in third-person plural verb forms.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaiden2016500-70)
* Final *-s* (including *-x*) in a large number of morphological endings (verb endings *-ās/-ēs/-īs/-is*, *-mus*, *-tis*; nominative singular *-us/-is*; plural *-ās/-ōs/-ēs*) and certain other words (*trēs* "three", *sex* "six", *crās* "tomorrow", etc.).
* Final *-n* in some monosyllables (often from earlier *-m*).
* Final *-r*, *-d* in some prepositions (e.g. *ad*, *per*), which were [clitics](/wiki/Clitic "Clitic")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] that attached phonologically to the following word.
* Very occasionally, final *-c*, e.g. [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") *oc* "yes" < *hoc*, [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *avuec* "with" < *apud hoc* (although these instances were possibly protected by a final [epenthetic](/wiki/Epenthesis "Epenthesis") vowel at one point).

Final *-t* was eventually lost in many languages, although this often occurred several centuries after the Vulgar Latin period. For example, the reflex of *-t* was dropped in [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") and [Old Spanish](/wiki/Old_Spanish "Old Spanish") only around 1100. In Old French, this occurred only when a vowel still preceded the *t* (generally /ə/ < Latin *a*). Hence *amat* "he loves" > Old French *aime* but *venit* "he comes" > Old French *vient*: the /t/ was never dropped and survives into Modern French in [liaison](/wiki/Liaison_(French) "Liaison (French)"), e.g. *vient-il?* "is he coming?" /vjɛ̃ti(l)/ (the corresponding /t/ in *aime-t-il?* is analogical, not inherited). Old French also kept the third-person plural ending *-nt* intact.

In Italo-Romance and the [Eastern Romance languages](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages"), eventually all final consonants were either lost or protected by an epenthetic vowel, except for some articles and a few monosyllabic prepositions *con*, *per*, *in*. Modern Standard Italian still has very few consonant-final words, although Romanian has regained them through later loss of final /u/ and /i/. For example, *amās* "you love" > *ame* > Italian *ami*; *amant* "they love" > \**aman* > Ital. *amano*. On the evidence of "sloppily written" [Lombardic language](/wiki/Lombardic_language "Lombardic language") documents, however, the loss of final /s/ in northern Italy did not occur until the 7th or 8th century, after the Vulgar Latin period, and the presence of many former final consonants is betrayed by the [syntactic gemination](/wiki/Syntactic_gemination "Syntactic gemination") (*raddoppiamento sintattico*) that they trigger. It is also thought that after a long vowel /s/ became /j/ rather than simply disappearing: *nōs* > *noi* "we", *crās* > *crai* "tomorrow" (southern Italy).[[71]](#cite_note-71) In unstressed syllables, the resulting diphthongs were simplified: *canēs* > \*/ˈkanej/ > *cani* "dogs"; *amīcās* > \*/aˈmikaj/ > *amiche* /aˈmike/ "(female) friends", where nominative *amīcae* should produce *\*\*amice* rather than *amiche* (note masculine *amīcī* > *amici* not *\*amichi*).

Central [Western Romance languages](/wiki/Western_Romance_languages "Western Romance languages") eventually regained a large number of final consonants through the general loss of final /e/ and /o/, e.g. Catalan *llet* "milk" < *lactem*, *foc* "fire" < *focum*, *peix* "fish" < *piscem*. In French, most of these secondary final consonants (as well as primary ones) were lost before around 1700, but tertiary final consonants later arose through the loss of /ə/ < *-a*. Hence masculine *frīgidum* "cold" > Old French *froit* /'frwεt/ > *froid* /fʁwa/, feminine *frīgidam* > Old French *froide* /'frwεdə/ > *froide* /fʁwad/.

#### Palatalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Palatalization")]

Main article: [Palatalization in the Romance languages](/wiki/Palatalization_in_the_Romance_languages "Palatalization in the Romance languages")

In Romance languages the term 'palatalization' is used to describe the phonetic evolution of velar stops preceding a front vowel and of consonant clusters involving yod or of the palatal approximant itself.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The process involving gestural blending and articulatory reinforcement, starting from [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin") and Early Romance, generated a new series of consonants in Romance languages.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

#### Lenition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Lenition")]

[Stop consonants](/wiki/Stop_consonant "Stop consonant") shifted by [lenition](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") in Vulgar Latin in some areas.

The voiced [labial consonants](/wiki/Labial_consonant "Labial consonant") /b/ and /w/ (represented by ⟨b⟩ and ⟨v⟩, respectively) both developed a [fricative](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_fricative "Voiced bilabial fricative") [β] as an intervocalic allophone.[[74]](#cite_note-74) This is clear from the orthography; in medieval times, the spelling of a consonantal ⟨v⟩ is often used for what had been a ⟨b⟩ in Classical Latin, or the two spellings were used interchangeably. In many Romance languages (Italian, French, Portuguese, Romanian, etc.), this fricative later developed into a /v/; but in others (Spanish, Galician, some Catalan and Occitan dialects, etc.) reflexes of /b/ and /w/ simply merged into a single phoneme.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkireRosen201032–33-75)

Several other consonants were "softened" in intervocalic position in Western Romance (Spanish, Portuguese, French, Northern Italian), but normally not phonemically in the rest of Italy (except some cases of "elegant" or Ecclesiastical words),[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] nor apparently at all in Romanian. The dividing line between the two sets of dialects is called the [La Spezia–Rimini Line](/wiki/La_Spezia%E2%80%93Rimini_Line "La Spezia–Rimini Line") and is one of the most important [isogloss](/wiki/Isogloss "Isogloss") bundles of the Romance dialects.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The changes (instances of diachronic lenition resulting in [phonological restructuring](/wiki/Phonological_change "Phonological change")) are as follows:
Single voiceless plosives became [voiced](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)"): *-p-, -t-, -c-* > *-b-, -d-, -g-*. Subsequently, in some languages they were further weakened, either becoming [fricatives](/wiki/Fricative "Fricative") or [approximants](/wiki/Approximant_consonant "Approximant consonant"), [β̞], [ð̞], [ɣ˕] (as in Spanish) or disappearing entirely (such as /t/ and /k/ lost between vowels in French, but /p/ > /v/). The following example shows progressive weakening of original /t/: e.g. *vītam* > Italian *vita* [ˈviːta], Portuguese *vida* [ˈvidɐ] (European Portuguese [ˈviðɐ]), Spanish *vida* [ˈbiða] (Southern Peninsular Spanish [ˈbi.a]), and French *vie* [vi]. Some scholars have speculated that these sound changes may be due in part to the influence of [Continental Celtic languages](/wiki/Continental_Celtic_languages "Continental Celtic languages"),[[77]](#cite_note-77) while scholarship of the past few decades has proposed internal motivations.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

* The voiced plosives /d/ and /ɡ/ tended to disappear.
* The plain [sibilant](/wiki/Sibilant_consonant "Sibilant consonant") *-s-* [s] was also voiced to [z] between vowels, although in many languages its spelling has not changed. (In Spanish, intervocalic [z] was later devoiced back to [s]; [z] is found only as an [allophone](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of /s/ before voiced consonants in Modern Spanish.)
* The [double](/wiki/Geminate_consonant "Geminate consonant") plosives became single: *-pp-, -tt-, -cc-, -bb-, -dd-, -gg-* > *-p-, -t-, -c-, -b-, -d-, -g-* in most languages. Subsequently, in some languages the voiced forms were further weakened, either becoming fricatives or approximants, [β̞], [ð̞], [ɣ˕] (as in Spanish). In French spelling, double consonants are merely etymological, except for -ll- after -i (pronounced [ij]), in most cases.
* The double sibilant *-ss-* [sː] also became phonetically and phonemically single [s], although in many languages its spelling has not changed. Double sibilant remains in some [languages of Italy](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy"), like Italian, Sardinian, and Sicilian.

The sound /h/ was lost but later reintroduced into individual Romance languages. The so-called *h aspiré* "aspirated h" in French, now completely silent, was a borrowing from [Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language"). In Spanish, word-initial /f/ changed to /h/ during its [Medieval stage](/wiki/Old_Spanish "Old Spanish") and was lost afterwards (for example *farina* > *harina*).[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkireRosen201034-79) Romanian acquired it most likely from the adstrate.[[80]](#cite_note-Sala-80)

[Consonant length](/wiki/Geminate_consonant "Geminate consonant") is no longer phonemically distinctive in most Romance languages. However some [languages of Italy](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy") (Italian, [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language"), Sicilian, and numerous other varieties of central and southern Italy) do have long consonants like /bb/, /dd/, /ɡɡ/, /pp/, /tt/, /kk/, /ll/, /mm/, /nn/, /rr/, /ss/, etc., where the doubling indicates either actual length or, in the case of [plosives](/wiki/Plosive_consonants "Plosive consonants") and [affricates](/wiki/Affricate "Affricate"), a short hold before the consonant is released, in many cases with distinctive lexical value: e.g. *note* /ˈnɔte/ (notes) vs. *notte* /ˈnɔtte/ (night), *cade* /ˈkade/ (s/he, it falls) vs. *cadde* /ˈkadde/ (s/he, it fell), *caro* /ˈkaro/ (dear, expensive) vs. *carro* /ˈkarro/ (cart, car). They may even occur at the beginning of words in [Romanesco](/wiki/Romanesco_dialect "Romanesco dialect"), Neapolitan, Sicilian and other southern varieties, and are occasionally indicated in writing, e.g. Sicilian *cchiù* (more), and *ccà* (here). In general, the consonants /b/, /ts/, and /dz/ are long at the start of a word, while the [archiphoneme](/wiki/Archiphoneme "Archiphoneme") |R|[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Romance_languages#Dubious "Talk:Romance languages")*] is realised as a [trill](/wiki/Trill_consonant "Trill consonant") /r/ in the same position. In much of central and southern Italy, the affricates /tʃ/ and /dʒ/ weaken synchronically to fricative [ʃ] and [ʒ] between vowels, while their geminate congeners do not, e.g. *cacio* /ˈkatʃo/ → [ˈkaːʃo] (cheese) vs. *caccio* /ˈkattʃo/ → [ˈkattʃo] (I chase). In Italian the geminates /ʃʃ/, /ɲɲ/, and /ʎʎ/ are pronounced as long [ʃʃ], [ɲɲ], and [ʎʎ] between vowels, but normally reduced to short following pause: *lasciare* 'let, leave' or *la sciarpa* 'the scarf' with [ʃʃ], but post-pausal *sciarpa* with [ʃ].

A few languages have regained secondary geminate consonants. The double consonants of [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language") exist only after stressed /ə/, written *ë*, and are not etymological: *vëdde* (Latin *vidēre*, to see), *sëcca* (Latin *sicca*, dry, feminine of *sech*). In standard Catalan and Occitan, there exists a geminate sound /lː/ written *ŀl* (Catalan) or *ll* (Occitan), but it is usually pronounced as a simple sound in colloquial (and even some formal) speech in both languages.

#### Vowel prosthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romance_languages&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Vowel prosthesis")]

In [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin") a [prosthetic](/wiki/Prothesis_(linguistics) "Prothesis (linguistics)") vowel /i/ (lowered to /e/ in most languages) was inserted at the beginning of any word that began with /s/ (referred to as *s impura*) and a voiceless consonant (#sC- > isC-):[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkireRosen201026-81)

* *scrībere* 'to write' > Sardinian *iscribere*, Spanish *escribir*, Portuguese *escrever*, Catalan *escriure*, Old French *escri(v)re* (mod. *écrire*);
* *spatha* "sword" > Sard *ispada*, Sp/Pg *espada*, Cat *espasa*, OFr *espeḍe* (modern *épée*);
* *spiritus* "spirit" > Sard *ispìritu*, Sp *espíritu*, Pg *espírito*, Cat *esperit*, French *esprit*;
* *Stephanum* "Stephen" > Sard *Istèvene*, Sp *Esteban*, Cat *Esteve*, Pg *Estêvão*, OFr *Estievne* (mod. *Étienne*);
* *status* "state" > Sard *istadu*, Sp/Pg *estado*, Cat *estat*, OFr *estat* (mod. *état*).

While Western Romance words fused the prosthetic vowel with the word, cognates in Eastern Romance and southern Italo-Romance did not, e.g. Italian *scrivere*, *spada*, *spirito*, *Stefano*, and *stato*, Romanian *scrie*, *spată*, *spirit*, *Ștefan* and *stat*. In Italian, syllabification rules were preserved instead by vowel-final articles, thus feminine *spada* as *la spada*, but instead of rendering the masculine *\*il stato*, *lo stato* came to be the norm. Though receding at present, Italian once had a prosthetic /i/ maintaining /s/ syllable-final if a consonant preceded such clusters, so that 'in Switzerland' was *in* [i]*Svizzera*. Some speakers still use the prothetic [i] productively, and it is fossilized in a few set locutions such as *in ispecie* 'especially' or *per iscritto* 'in writing' (a form whose survival may have been buttressed in part by the word *iscritto* < Latin *īnscrīptus*).

| *o (ŏ)* | short *o* | /ɔ/ | /ɔ/ | /o/ | **ǫ** | \*/ɔ/ | /ɔ/ | /ɔ/ |
| *ō* | long *o* | /oː/ | **ọ** | \*/o/ | /o/ | /u/ |
| *au* *(a few words)* | *au* | /aw/ > /ɔː/ |
| *u (ŭ)* | short *u* | /ʊ/ | /u/ | /u/ | **ų** | \*/ʊ/ |
| *ū* | long *u* | /uː/ | **ụ** | \*/u/ | /u/ |
| *au* *(most words)* | *au* | /aw/ | /aw/ | /aw/ | **au** | \*/aw/ | /aw/ | /aw/ |
| 1 Traditional academic transcription in Latin and [Romance studies](/wiki/Romance_studies "Romance studies"), respectively. | | | | | | | | |

One profound change that affected Vulgar Latin was the reorganisation of its [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") system.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkireRosen20108-82) Classical Latin had five short vowels, *ă, ĕ, ĭ, ŏ, ŭ*, and five [long vowels](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length"), *ā, ē, ī, ō, ū*, each of which was an individual [phoneme](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") (see the table in the right, for their likely pronunciation in IPA), and four [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"), *ae*, *oe*, *au* and *eu* (five according to some authors, including *ui*). There were also long and short versions of *y*, representing the [rounded vowel](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel") /y(ː)/ in Greek borrowings, which however probably came to be pronounced /i(ː)/ even before Romance vowel changes started.

There is evidence that in the imperial period all the short vowels except *a* differed by quality as well as by length from their long counterparts.[[83]](#cite_note-83) So, for example *ē* was pronounced [close-mid](/wiki/Close-mid_vowel "Close-mid vowel") /eː/ while *ĕ* was pronounced [open-mid](/wiki/Open-mid_vowel "Open-mid vowel") /ɛ/, and *ī* was pronounced [close](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") /iː/ while *ĭ* was pronounced [near-close](/wiki/Near-close_vowel "Near-close vowel") /ɪ/.

During the Proto-Romance period, phonemic length distinctions were lost. Vowels came to be automatically pronounced long in stressed, [open syllables](/wiki/Open_syllable "Open syllable") (i.e. when followed by only one consonant), and pronounced short everywhere else. This situation is still maintained in modern Italian: *cade* [ˈkaːde] "he falls" vs. *cadde* [ˈkadde] "he fell".

The Proto-Romance loss of phonemic length originally produced a system with nine different quality distinctions in monophthongs, where only original /a aː/ had merged.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlkireRosen201013-84) Soon, however, many of these vowels coalesced:

* The simplest outcome was in [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language"),[[85]](#cite_note-85) where the former long and short vowels in Latin simply coalesced, e.g. /ɛ eː/ > /ɛ/, /ɪ iː/ > /i/: This produced a simple five-vowel system /a ɛ i ɔ u/.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELedgewayMaiden2016471-86)
* In most areas, however (technically, the [Italo-Western languages](/wiki/Italo-Western_languages "Italo-Western languages")), the near-close vowels /ɪ ʊ/ lowered and merged into the high-mid vowels /e o/. As a result, Latin *pira* "pear" and *vēra* "true", came to rhyme (e.g. Italian and Spanish *pera, vera*, and [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *poire, voire*). Similarly, Latin *nucem* (from *nux* "nut") and *vōcem* (from *vōx* "voice") become Italian *noce, voce*, Portuguese *noz, voz*, and French *noix, voix*. This produced a seven-vowel system /a ɛ e i ɔ o u/, still maintained in conservative languages such as Italian and Portuguese, and lightly transformed in Spanish (where /ɛ/ > /je/, /ɔ/ > /we/).
* In the [Eastern Romance languages](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages") (particularly, [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language")), the front vowels /ĕ ē ĭ ī/ evolved as in the majority of languages, but the back vowels /ɔ oː ʊ uː/ evolved as in Sardinian. This produced an unbalanced six-vowel system: /a ɛ e i o u/. In modern Romanian, this system has been significantly transformed, with /ɛ/ > /je/ and with new vowels /ə ɨ/ evolving, leading to a balanced seven-vowel system with central as well as front and back vowels: /a e i ə ɨ o u/.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESala2012146-87)
* [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language") is sometimes described as having its own distinct vowel system. In fact, Sicilian passed through the same developments as the main bulk of Italo-Western languages. Subsequently, however, high-mid vowels (but not low-mid vowels) were raised in all syllables, stressed and unstressed; i.e. /e o/ > /i u/. The result is a five-vowel /a ɛ i ɔ u/.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELedgewayMaiden2016471-86)

Further variants are found in southern Italy and Corsica, which also boasts a completely distinct system.

Outcome of stressed Classical Latin vowels in dialects of southern Italy, Sardinia and Corsica[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| **ĕ**, **ae** | \*/ɛ/ | /ɛ/ | /e/ | /ɛ/ | /ɛ/ | /ɛ/ | /ɛ/ | /ɛ/ | /e/ | /e/ | /ɛ/ | /e/ (/ɛ/) |
| **ē**, **oe** | \*/e/ | /e/ | /i/ | /ɪ/ (/ɛ/) | /e/ | /e/ |
